He considered Yoga to be the immortal soul of this subcontinent, where new bodies had the opportunity to fulfill the purpose of incarnation: to find the Spirit within oneself and to realize (or actualize)

...it.

And he firmly believed that a person must complete himself in every respect in order to be transformed through the process of transformation. Having united with the soil, the seed grows into a tree; to assume a new form, it must cease to be a seed. In the same way, the search and investigation of the inner “self” transforms the seeker. A person is forced to adapt to a rapidly changing world, and adaptation stimulates inner metamorphoses. The soil for spiritual growth is fertilized by the past grandeur of many religions; any revision of belief systems inevitably conditions the beginning of a new cycle.

…The strangest thing about people is that they cannot truly feel time. A person simply knows that history continues to change. This perception is purely intellectual, since life is too short to contain and understand all aspects of change. In the absence of comparable experience, one must rely on historical archives and one’s own imagination. For most young people, events of 60 years ago and 600 years ago are largely similar—they all happened long ago, and are therefore perceived as abstractly relevant and alike. The sensation of Time is blurred mainly due to the loss of traditions. Throughout history, religion has provided the most significant landmarks. Now, spirituality is becoming the marker of Time’s flow. What will it orient itself toward? The internal

timekeeper exists thanks to the body, which gathers data by observing the aging of others and follows established and expected processes, meekly allowing time to seep into bone and flesh. And could it be otherwise?

…Many years ago, he left home, abandoned his accustomed way of life, and chose the path of yoga. Deconstructing habitual patterns and predictable narratives, yoga led him toward a gradual understanding of the continuity and wholeness of Time. Studying the world through the lens of Kriya, he learned to observe the collisions of old and new, the struggle of different principles, the battles for resources and minds—all of which were the engine of humanity’s progress. These cycles are quite bloody, yet Time is least concerned with pain. As a true Scientist, It observes the birth of worlds and new species, continuing to assemble and disassemble the universal construction set.

…He did not try to guess whether humanity would develop a broader understanding of reality or whether new religious, mystical, and apocalyptic meanings would prevail. One thing is clear: spiritual awakening is destined to become the dominant trend. Along with the changing world, new codes of the sacred arose. Myriads of inner universes awaited an awakened mind capable of discovering them.

**The Lottery and the Prize**

Assumptions that enlightened masters hold a protective shroud of their prayers over the planet are exaggerated. As far as he could tell, prayers (collective or individual) exert a therapeutic effect exclusively on those who pray. The fact that during his time in samadhi he never encountered other realized mortal masters in the astral world proved nothing. Perhaps they worked in other “astral departments.” In general, there is no one to welcome and protect newly arrived souls, and over time they drifted toward whatever egregore they had been drawn to in their past life. Souls relied mainly on the help of living relatives and rituals performed for their pacification. The laws of the subtle worlds are complex and harsh, but even they are subject to radical changes.

Shifts in the astral hierarchy are akin to corporate appointments and resignations; each new ruler brings his own team. In the time of the Mahabharata, avatars and children of Deities born from earthly women (such as Arjuna, Karna, Krishna) carried out the will of the rulers of the subtle worlds. Some of these “double agents” knew of their tasks and represented the interests of the gods consciously (or against their will). Most obeyed the call, fulfilling their des-

tiny and initiating significant changes in the world. Besides them, in all eras the ark of civilization remained afloat thanks to murderers and scientists, inventors and poets, dictators and dreamers who influenced the course of events. The role of the individual in history was determined not only by karmas and intentions, but also by algorithms programmed by higher forces.

No, he had not watched too many science fiction films, but merely observed and from time to time communicated with various spirits, ghosts, and their masters. The life of astral inhabitants is as complex as their motives and goals. Over the last thirty years, he had spoken about the reshuffling in the hierarchy of astral rulers and how these shifts affected events on Earth. It was entirely unimportant if some interpreted these views as extravagant theories. Never trying to convince or impress anyone, he was content with his personal understanding of the complex system of world order, which continued to reveal itself to him.

The struggle of celestial entities for dominance exerted a strong influence on human affairs, since the planet is a transit hub where six different timelines and countless dimensions intersect and merge. Astral beings came here for various reasons. Some were fugitive criminals who, trying to avoid their sentences, were born as humans. These extra-

terrestrial offenders were meant to die during wars or as a result of natural disasters, so that their souls could be “deported” back to their worlds. However fantastic this may sound, mass deportations of such souls are not uncommon.

Earth is not merely part of a portfolio of celestial real estate. There was something about this planet that made it a center of unflagging interest and attention for immortals, gods, lower and higher spirits, and demons. For the gods, She is a deity, a conscious being whose power and energy allowed even the highest spirits to incarnate. Astral rulers cared for Earth and revered Her because She was everything at once—a giant womb and the Garden of Eden, a laboratory and a refuge for refugees, a prison and a university, a vast cinema and a torture chamber. For the inhabitants of the subtle worlds, humans are merely livestock carrying out a continuous program of reproduction, since spirits constantly need new bodies.

Recently, however, planned reproduction has spiraled out of control. Resources were sufficient, but the logistics of incarnations and timely departures were faltering. One thing is clear: whoever controls Earth inevitably rules the subtle worlds as well. The Indian epics provided many hints and allusions confirming this fact. The identities of the players are always hidden, but the prize is great.

…After the Second World War, the world changed; sig-

nificant shifts occurred, stimulated by people’s hopes for healing and recovery. And then, on September 11, 2001, another turning point in modern history took place. The vulnerability of Western society was exposed, its values challenged. And yet, capitalism as a system continued to prove its effectiveness and viability.

The world had not yet fully recovered from the shock of the pandemic when 2022 began rattling quite real, albeit rusty, weapons. A revision of civilizational frameworks began anew, with military conflicts in Eastern Europe and the Middle East, grotesquely reflecting events of the early twentieth century. Geopolitical transitions never occur without fertile ground, without hidden or overt causes. Dictatorships and autocracies fight for survival, attacking a weakened democracy that, having lost vigilance over years of prosperity, had nearly forgotten the science of war, confrontation, and self-defense.

It cannot be said that this confrontation was so inevitable. Like countless wars of the past, the current conflicts marked changes intended to reset and accelerate the development of society, to revise the spiritual and moral paradigm. The battle of Kurukshetra began anew; brothers and relatives killed each other, and the struggle between archaism and modernity could drag on indefinitely.

In the subtle worlds, this organized tragedy was perceived as one of countless combinations in a giant Nintendo. The betting pools of the future were spinning again for millions of people, and no winners were in sight. The birth pangs of the old world were becoming more frequent, and the birth of a new era promised to be difficult.

Having clearly delineated the foundations of morality, ethics, and spiritual and moral views, the transition of epochs sharpened dualism, allowing no multi-variant interpretations. And yet, he urged students to see a broader picture of the world, to realize the global nature of the changes taking place, and to live through them consciously. And, as a spiritual mentor, he supported all disciples in moments of pain, darkness, and struggle.

There was never absolute predetermination in history. Its course was determined by collective karma, composed of mosaics of intentions and actions of individuals, communities, and countries. As always, all of this was filtered through the divine will of Time itself.

**Remaining Desires**

One of the exquisite pleasures of life is to forget one’s “self,” floating in the ocean of thoughtlessness, where no memories of past personalities dwell. Kriya led to another kind of satisfaction: remembering one’s own essence, realizing the wholeness of one’s “self” as the sum of experiences from past incarnations. The magical kaleidoscope of this yoga revealed concentrated clusters and contours of elusive Time, allowing a glimpse into the astonishing diversity of worlds. Of course, this is not a panacea for the inevitable finitude of bodily incarnation. The relentless millstones of Time indiscriminately ground both the subtle and the coarse, seasoning the grind with the bittersweet pollen of emotions and unfulfilled desires.

Everything and everyone is created from the same substance, saturated with the same pranic force: the reaper and the grain, the miller and the millstones. All forms are transient, but prana, Time, and Emptiness live in every object and being, awaiting transformation, if not recognition. One life is insufficient for a full inventory of these concepts, simultaneously familiar and eternally alien to the mind. In the end, the mind would abandon attempts to grasp the meaning of the infinite transformation of substances, objects, and seasons; dropping all objectivity, thought would defocus, only to experience a blind-

ing explosion of Truth. This usually happens when a mature consciousness is ready to absorb the new.

…At times it seemed that *smashan* triumphed over Time, and His tangled nets were left to soak in a pond, where apathetic fish and indifferent ducks pecked at them. Then Time was hung to dry on stone balustrades, and ghosts sometimes fashioned hammocks or camouflage veils from His torn cloud-fibers. Years passed, and suddenly someone would appear with grown children, or even grandchildren. The blushing youth of yesterday was about to retire. The former chubby little girl who used to come with her parents now held her own baby in her arms. The old tree in front of the house had grown threefold, and the fourth generation of German Shepherds chased the same monkey through the overgrown garden. The photographic film of life developed slowly, in the old way. The emerging contours and forms were recognizable, yet the essence remained hidden.

…Straightening his legs from padmasana, he waited for the numbness to subside; the years spent in the lotus posture resonated in his muscles with a tingling sensation. His gait was steady; daily exercises with dumbbells, barbells, and mudgars kept his body in excellent shape, according to a pact once made. His hair was still coal-black, and only in the stubble on his chin did gray appear. Shaving made this marker of time insignificant. Apart from these nuances, he felt the fluid elusiveness of Time rather

...like the changing of seasons, rather than as a destructive force. The peculiarity of age (simultaneously a curse and a blessing) lies in the fact that from a certain point, everything seems a reflection of something already seen or experienced.

The diversity of human archetypes, events, and the interconnections between them can be perceived as something repetitive, but each combination has a special zest, a unique fragrance. Everything passed through the filters of Time, everything was dissected and studied, ultimately submitting to His supremacy. And the process of aging is the most effective method of knowing Time. Perhaps the awareness of His movement was the most important part of the soul's education. Often the question of the finiteness of all things remained suspended: "Let's see where this leads us." Any assertions are banal. All living beings moved toward an invisible line. The transition to bodilessness awaited everyone.

...Peace, obscurity, and the absence of cares are considered the best friends of any person. Befriending them is not difficult; one need only pass the mind through the sieve of the present, leaving behind all memories and projections. Thus a river glides over pebbles, leaving grains of precious metal in the gold prospector's sluice as a reward for having witnessed its flow. But moments of complete peace are brief, even for the renounced.

To those around him, his days appeared serene, yet his duties were relentless. *Sādhana* required a clear schedule and unwavering concentration; disciples needed attention; and household affairs demanded flexibility and timely planning. Other duties were never mentioned, for they were connected with astral events. It seemed that changes in the external world had not the slightest influence on the inhabitants of the *smashan*, as if its residents lived according to a different time scale. The existential toxicity of being pierced the invisible shield of the *smashan*, and only the nectar of silent bliss filled the stone honeycombs of the cenotaphs and spread through the intricate network of garden paths.

Over the years, his emotions became refined, elevated, even exquisite—and pain and despair would have become so as well, had he allowed them entry. This is precisely why he never ventured beyond the territory—there is too much pain in the world. But how sensitive was he to the suffering of others? Sometimes people in the room noticed that his answers echoed their own thoughts, that they were composed of the very words just uttered in their minds. Did this mean he read thoughts, and if so, did he feel their emotions? Yes, if he permitted himself to.

Once, during *darśana*, involuntarily touching his forehead, he glanced at his fingers: they were smeared with blood. In bewilderment, he scanned those present. Someone's phone rang, and a family from Bengal received alarming news: their nephew had just been in a car accident, and the rearview mirror had split open his forehead. Such sporadic episodes were inexplicable, and he had to learn to suppress these attacks of "shared pain," while still allowing the flow of compassion to manifest. Responding to desperate pleas, he could take on others' illnesses and paid for this literally, with the loss of his own weight and health.

People died en masse and daily. Did he feel the waves of their suffering, hear their final thoughts—or did he consciously block all this, since such sensitivity could break even the most steadfast mind? How did he manage to guard his heart from the impact of mass despair, from the pain of thousands of trembling and sobbing souls?

Yes, he could feel and hear their pleas; they begged for help, asked him to show them the way. Sometimes, if they were somehow connected to segments of recognizable reality in which he could discern the causes and results of their *karmas*, he responded.

...The system of comprehending the world through self-knowledge, *Kriyā* was simultaneously simple and complex, and he never ceased to marvel at the precision of this science. It burned away illusions, granted an understanding of the global interconnection of things, elements, and forces, and an awareness of one's place in the complex symbiosis of all processes of Life. It also removed uncertainty, since everything and everyone had its place and purpose—both here, on Earth, and there, on the astral plane.

His own mission had long been clear to him—to continue this *yoga*, to preserve the tradition, and to awaken those who sought to give meaning to nearly forgotten dreams and memories of their true Self. To lead them toward new incarnations or toward final liberation. As for the desires that remained, there were few: to continue listening to the lullabies of the Earth, floating in the embrace of the Void, and to remain with the Great Mother forever.

**Flame and Embers**

In the winter of 2008, in the midst of a lively discussion, he suddenly said with a smile: "If you decide to write about me, mention the following: 'This book is about a coward who was so afraid of the external world that he preferred life on the *smashan*...'" Despite the playful tone of the statement, everything was precisely the opposite. Living at the cremation ground required greater courage and harsher discipline than navigating the treacherous and murky, yet in many ways predictable, waters of the great *samsāric* ocean stretching beyond the gates. The abode of Śiva and Śakti, the *smashan* insisted on the most severe honesty with oneself and unconditional compassion for all.

The teaching received here transformed the minds and destinies of those who took initiation into this *yoga* seriously. One need not be a believer to believe, nor religious to pray, for the song of the heart has no need for vocal cords. All that is expected of people coming to this Teacher is sincerity of intention and openness of heart.

In the last 30 years, he managed to survive and thrive where only suffering and despair could dwell. By dedicating his life to this *yoga* and this land, he proved the opposite.

He himself believes that Babaji did not accidentally lead him, the last continuer of the lineage, to the *smashan*, to the place where all earthly journeys end. It may be that the authentic version of *Kriyā* will be withdrawn, suspended, and its return may take several centuries, as has happened before. Meanwhile, both he and the *smashan* continue to exist unnoticed in the shadow of far more significant forces and events.

The Director of the Siddha Siddhanta Academy deliberately avoids any self-promotion. There is no need to attract even more pilgrims and devotees. Guided by the invisible compass of *karma*, all who are destined to practice this *yoga* will find their own way to him. The land of liberation, *Mokṣa Bhūmi*, is securely hidden from view. One can walk past the old gates without even suspecting that the greatest modern yogi-mystic lives right here, behind them.

The metaphysical abysses of the *smashan* are concealed just as *Suṣumṇā*[^1] is concealed within the body. This mystical channel possesses the potential to open to the ascending splendor of *Kuṇḍalinī*, towards the light of a thousand suns. *Suṣumṇā* exists in everyone, but only a few can activate it. Everyone eventually comes to the *smashan*, but only a few can accept the invitation to live there, teach, and worship the gods. The place is intended for *yoga* and discipline, for spirits and gods, for the souls of the dead and for some of the living.

Almost everything in the life of this biography's hero happened contrary to circumstances. His life has been tested and tempered by the acid fumes of public ostracism, and continues to be tested. Not by people, but by Time itself. The stories of his adventures need no one's recognition or admiration, and these narratives have never been softened for skeptics or critics. What happened, happened—that is all. Disbelief, doubt, and criticism of these narratives are quite natural, as they are part of a greater inquiry. In essence, any interesting conversations and engaging debates are welcome here. And yet, no one has yet dared to venture into the astral, into the subtle worlds—and return with sufficient experience to ask detailed questions about the specifics of *samādhi*. So far, Shailendra Sharma awaits the aspiring yogis of realization to tell their own stories (undoubtedly no less captivating than his own).

…What will become of this place in 30, 80, 200 years? Who will perform the nightly *pūjā*, blow the bull's horn, light the *dhunī*, and beat the drums, invoking Śiva? Is there someone destined to continue this tradition, to awaken new seekers to the infinity of lives, not necessarily earthly? Will this Guru await his disciples there, in the light between lives?

A ritual is significant only as long as it is performed. This mystical place still calls those who are destined to come. Or releases them, following the command of unknown forces.

Only Babaji holds the answers; only the *paramparā* decides the fate of the next Teacher. Succession is permitted within the Teacher's family, but such a spiritual tradition usually ends after three generations. Guru Shailendra Sharma prefers to know, rather than to guess. Whatever his thoughts about the future, he keeps them to himself.

Nine years have passed since he began the special practice prescribed by Babaji. The first effects of this *yoga* may become noticeable after 12 years. Who knows, perhaps the results of the practice are the very condition for the next meeting with Mahavatar? *Kriyā* proved to be a most powerful evolutionary tool, although its mystery remains unsolved, despite the increase in the number of practitioners. Conjectures, hints, and personal discoveries define the general direction, but many still wonder: how does this path differ from religion?

The knowledge of one's own Spirit opens the possibility of a trusting connection between the soul and its bearer, between *Ātmā* and *Ātman*—until the Spirit itself becomes the Teacher for the practitioner. The inner deity lives in everyone, and over time, the power of the strengthened human spirit will replace organized religions. The Kingdom of the Spirit is here, on Earth.

At some point, people will be unable to move forward in their development without a higher purpose, and it would be too primitive to continue considering accumulation and consumption as the main meaning of life. What discoveries will help humanity evolve? Sooner or later, the main component of the Universe, dark matter, will be deciphered—but the knowledge of ourselves will become the greatest revelation for all sentient beings.

The fire of this mystical *yoga*, kindled many centuries ago, may eventually die down, but the embers always continue to smolder, preserving the essence of the fire.

**Gratitude**

[^1]: *Smashan* and *Suṣumṇā* – from conversations with Shri Shailendra Sharma: "Haṭha Yoga Pradīpikā compares *Suṣumṇā* with the *smashan* – and this is a serious topic for reflection for all who seek to understand the essence of *kriyā*. Haṭha Yoga Pradīpikā states: '*Suṣumṇā*, *Śūnya*, *Padavī*, *Brahma Randhra*, *Mahā Patha*, *Smasāna*, *Sambhavī*, *Madhya Mārga* – these are names of the same thing... *Suṣumṇā* becomes the main road for the passage of *Prāṇa*, and the mind becomes free from all bonds (objects of enjoyment), and then one can avoid Death...' Advanced yogis believe that *Suṣumṇā* is actually the *smashan*, and there are metaphysical parallels in this assertion. One of the terms for *Suṣumṇā* is '*smashan*', the place where Śiva and Śakti dwell. Interestingly, *Prāṇa* is one of the names of Śiva, and *Apāna* is one of the names of Śakti. When *Prāṇa* and *Apāna* merge, they are ready to enter *Suṣumṇā*, to come to the *smashan*, where all worldly attachments are left behind, where you are dead to the world but awakened to spiritual spheres. If *Suṣumṇā* is 'awakened' (meaning *Prāṇa* and *Apāna* have entered it), then the astral world will be open to such a yogi. All spirits will recognize him, because, being alive, he, like any spirit, can navigate the astral world. *Suṣumṇā* is also called *Madhya Mārga* or the Middle Path. Perhaps when Gautama Buddha spoke of the Middle Path, he meant *Suṣumṇā*... Activation of the Middle Path (*Suṣumṇā*) means that consciousness will be awakened. Then the subconscious will become conscious, and one can enter a state of anabiosis or *samādhi*. Life at the *smashan* provides such a transformation."

This book was written on airplanes, at train stations, in trolleybuses, on riverbanks, in the New York City subway, on the back seat of a car racing along the bumpy roads of the Russian hinterland, on the sun-scorched plains of Uttar Pradesh, in the shade of the trees of Bangalore, and in the silence of the royal *smashan* at Govardhan. For writing these sketches and notes, two iPhones, two computers, one laptop, one fogged-up windowpane, beach sand, and several palm leaves in Gokarna were used.

The material for the book came from audio recordings of *darshans* and interviews with Guruji Shri Shailendra Sharma, collected over the last thirteen years. All the stories told here are true.

The mechanism of inscribing glyphs onto paper is simple, but the process of weaving a story from scattered words still evokes bewilderment in me. It seems it happened of its own accord.

I am forever grateful to my mother, my First Teacher, for the love and the life she gave me.

I express my deepest gratitude to Guru Shailendra Sharma, whose grace, life, and *sadhana* continue to guide hundreds of his students and friends.

Together with other students, over many years I have felt the support of Devahuti Pathak, Guruji’s sister, known as Mataji. With her blessing, the Guru’s home became our spiritual home.

I would like to express heartfelt gratitude to all who supported me in working on the book, contributed to it, and helped this project come to fruition: Danielle Landerville, my first reader and editor; Angelina Avetisova for her invaluable contribution to the design and editing of the first edition of the book; Olga Vatova, Padma Kulkarni, Natalia Kozhukhova, Satyajit Chakraborty, and Carly Buford for final editing and proofreading; Anastasia Leonova, who worked on the footnotes; Anju Kalra and Deepak Kumar for years of friendship and support; and also Sumit Jadia for the photographs and cover design ideas; Milan Atmanov for the retouching and proofreading of the photographs; Yulia Yakovleva for the layout of the Russian edition of the book—and to all those who continue to walk the path of yoga today.

…In search of the right words, we turn to the inkwell of the mind, while the heart composes silent lines of gratitude to the Guru. To the one who awakens us so that we may see the infinity of our spirit.

Jai Gurudev!

About the Author

Katya Mossin was born in Moscow and received a Master’s degree in Architecture from the Moscow Architectural Institute. Beginning in 1990, she worked in New York as an architect and urban planner, participating in numerous projects in the USA, Eastern Europe, and Asia. Thanks to her profession and her love of adventure, she has visited more than 50 countries.

In 2008, she began practicing Kriya Yoga under the guidance of Guru Shailendra Sharma. From 2011 to 2021, Katya conceptualized, structured, edited (and also translated into Russian) some of the works of Shri Shailendra Sharma: *At the Right Hand of God*, *The Twilight Language of Gorakh Bodh*, *The Upanishad of Immortality*.

Her stories have been published in the collection *Lost and Found: Stories from New York*. In 2021, she published *The Song of the Naked Heart*, a Russian translation of the poems of the Kashmiri yogini Lalla Ded with her own yogic commentaries. For the last 15 years, Katya Mossin has lived and worked in India. She currently teaches architecture as a visiting professor at Christ College in Bangalore.
